questions.question: Why is it recommended to write the Executive Summary LAST, even though it appears first?

options.text: Because it's the least important part of the proposal.
Because you can only accurately summarize the solution and outcomes after you've refined them in the body of the document.
Because it's usually the longest section to write.
Because executives prefer it to be written after business hours.

questions.explanation: The Executive Summary is a condensation of everything else in the proposal. You need to have the details of the solution and the ROI logic finalized before you can write a punchy, effective summary.

questions.question: What is the primary goal of the 'Situation Analysis' section?

options.text: To list your company's history and mission statement.
To demonstrate that you deeply understand the prospect's specific pain and the cost of their current problem.
To describe the technical architecture of your product.
To list the features you plan to build in the future.

questions.explanation: Trust is built when the prospect feels understood. Using their language and quantify the cost of their problem proves you were listening and that you are focused on their goals.

questions.question: According to the lesson, how should 'Features' be presented in the 'Proposed Solution' section?

options.text: As a comprehensive list alphabetically ordered.
As a technical specification document attached to the end.
Mapped directly to the specific problems/goals identified in the Situation Analysis.
Features shouldn't be mentioned at all; only outcomes.

questions.explanation: A feature without a corresponding 'why' is just noise. The most effective way to sell a feature is to explain exactly which pain point it eliminates.

questions.question: What is the 'Goldilocks' length for a B2B proposal for deals under $50,000?

options.text: 1 page (one-pager)
3-5 pages
10-15 pages to show thoroughness
As long as it takes to list every single feature

questions.explanation: 3-5 pages provides enough detail to be credible and persuasive without overwhelming the reader or creating a high cognitive load that leads to delay.

questions.question: Why should you avoid using words like 'Cost' or 'Price' in the final section?

options.text: Because they are too academic.
Because they frame the money as an expense to be minimized; 'Investment' frames it as capital deployed for a return.
Because those words are trademarked by competitors.
There is no reason; it's just personal preference.

questions.explanation: Psychological framing matters. An investment implies a future positive return, which makes the number easier to accept than a pure 'cost' which is just money leaving the pocket.

questions.question: Look at Section 3 (Proposed Solution). If you were to map your most powerful feature to a prospect's most painful problem using the formula 'Because you mentioned [Problem], we will use [Feature] to [Outcome],' what would that sentence look like for your business?
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate if the user follows the Problem -> Feature -> Outcome structure. Provide feedback on how to make the 'Outcome' part more quantifiable or emotional based on their input.
